\newcommand{\qth}{Q_\theta}
\newcommand{\qthone}{Q_{\theta,1}}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview}

This is the flagship Clean notebook in the XXX subproject. It sets up the two-site ($L=2$) rational
$\mathfrak{gl}(2)$ (XXX) spin chain --- independent representations $[\lambda_1,0]$, $[\lambda_2,0]$
at each site, independent inhomogeneities $\theta_1,\theta_2$, and a shared companion twist $G$ ---
and then builds up, across six sections (A through F, 71 cells total), the full machinery needed to
state and numerically verify a \emph{functional Clebsch--Gordan-overlap dictionary} for this chain.
Sections A and B were assembled on 2026-07-15 from two \texttt{Experiments/} notebooks
(\texttt{su2\_XXX\_L2.wb} and \texttt{Baxter\_Solver\_L2.wb}); Sections C--F were added on
2026-07-16, extending Sections A/B without modifying them:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Section A} (cells 2--26) --- GT-basis/generator machinery, Lax/monodromy/transfer
  matrices, the fusion (Hirota) hierarchy, the SoV $B$-operator, and the SoV pseudovacuum/covector
  basis, each followed by its own inline validation cell.
\item \textbf{Section B} (cells 27--34) --- the Baxter-equation ingredients, the genuine
  transfer-matrix-eigenstate solve, and the linear TQ solve for the numeric Baxter Q-function.
\item \textbf{Section C} (cells 35--45) --- the Functional Separation of Variables (FSoV) scalar
  product: per-site measures and a bracket pairing, a $2\times2$ determinant realizing
  $\langle Q_A,Q_B\rangle$, and a full Gram matrix over one representation, verified numerically
  diagonal. Ported from \texttt{Experiments/FSoV\_L2.wb}.
\item \textbf{Section D} (cells 46--52) --- left eigenvectors of the companion-twisted transfer
  matrix, paired with Section B's right eigenvectors to form the bilinear pairing
  $\langle\Psi|\Psi\rangle$, whose ratio against Section C's scalar product is found numerically
  equal to $1$.
\item \textbf{Section E} (cells 53--60) --- a standalone length-1 fused transfer-matrix hierarchy
  and SoV covector (the paper's genuine $L=1$ construction), used to prove that the $L=2$ SoV basis
  operatorially factorizes through a rank-1 site-2 passthrough eigenvalue.
\item \textbf{Section F} (cells 61--71) --- the headline result: a closed dictionary equating a
  functional object built from the numeric Baxter Q-functions with an operatorial object built by
  rotating the SoV pseudovacuum, verified to exact symbolic zero across 6 representations and 41
  states.
\end{itemize}

Every construction in Section A that has a direct $L=1$ counterpart is cross-referenced against
\texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2 (``The XXX spin chain and separation of variables''); the paper
currently stops at the single-site SoV pseudovacuum/covector construction (\S2.3), so
\textbf{none} of Sections B--F (Baxter/TQ solver, FSoV scalar product, left eigenvectors,
factorization, functional CG-overlap dictionary) has a paper counterpart yet --- this is new
material developed directly in the notebook, cross-referenced below against the design specs under
\url{docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-16-*} rather than against the paper.

As with the original A+B version of this notebook, this notebook's cell outputs currently render as
empty via the notebook-reading tools, across all six sections --- a documented tool-capture
artifact, confirmed unrelated to correctness by direct kernel-state inspection
(\texttt{wolfbook\_getKernelState}): every routine described below is present as a live
memoized/evaluated kernel value, consistent with the notebook's own inline hard-asserts (which
would abort on a genuine failure), and the recorded activity log shows a clean fresh-kernel
\texttt{Run All} immediately before this documentation pass. Every worked numeric example below is
therefore sourced from direct kernel-state inspection rather than a rendered cell output.

\paragraph{A note on notation.} The notebook itself uses literal Greek Unicode characters
($\lambda$, $\theta$, $\kappa$, $\chi$, $\tau$, $\mu$, $\varphi$) as Wolfram symbol names -- e.g.\
the representation labels are the Wolfram symbols whose names literally are $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$,
the inhomogeneities are $\theta_1,\theta_2$, the twist eigenvalues are $\kappa_1,\kappa_2$, the
Newton-identity coefficients of the twist are $\chi_1,\chi_2$, the transfer-matrix eigenvalue
function is named $\tau$, the FSoV measures are named $\mu$, and the unipotent rotation parameter is
named $\varphi$. Throughout this document these are rendered as their corresponding math-mode
macros rather than reproduced as literal glyphs; only Wolfram function calls with purely
Latin/numeric arguments (e.g.\ \texttt{TauEigensystem[1,1]}, \texttt{QSolve[2,1][2,0]}) are quoted
verbatim in \texttt{typewriter} font.

\section{The Gelfand--Tsetlin basis and single-site \texorpdfstring{$\mathfrak{gl}(2)$}{gl(2)} generators}
\label{sec:gt}

The single-site building blocks are ported verbatim from \texttt{Experiments/XXX\_su2.wb} and are
independent of the chain length $L$; every later construction in the notebook is built on top of
them. The Wolfram routine \texttt{ps[rep]} enumerates the Gelfand--Tsetlin (GT) patterns of a
rectangular $\mathfrak{gl}(N)$ irrep specified by a shape such as $\{\lambda,0\}$, memoized;
\texttt{dim[rep]} is simply the length of that list, i.e.\ the irrep dimension (memoized). For the
two-row $\mathfrak{gl}(2)$ shapes used throughout this notebook this gives the expected
$\dim V_\lambda = \lambda+1$: the notebook's own kernel state confirms $\dim\{1,0\}=2$,
$\dim\{2,0\}=3$, $\dim\{3,0\}=4$.

On top of this basis, \texttt{J0[k][pat]}, \texttt{Jp[k][pat]}, and \texttt{Jm[k][pat]} implement
the Cartan, raising, and lowering generator action on a single GT pattern \texttt{pat} at GT-row
level $k$, each returning a formal sum of basis vectors. The routine \texttt{JJ[k,l,rep]} assembles
these into the full matrix of the generator $E_{kl}$ (in the standard $\mathfrak{gl}(N)$ generator
convention) on \texttt{rep}'s GT basis: diagonal entries ($k=l$) and the entries adjacent to the
diagonal ($l=k\pm1$) are read directly from \texttt{Jp}/\texttt{Jm}, while all remaining entries are
built recursively from commutators of adjacent generators. As a representative evaluation, the
notebook's kernel state gives
\begin{verbatim}
JJ[1, 1, {2, 0}]
(* {{2, 0, 0}, {0, 1, 0}, {0, 0, 0}} *)
\end{verbatim}
i.e.\ the diagonal weight (Cartan) values $2,1,0$ for the three $\lambda=2$ GT basis states.

A thin wrapper, $E_{ij}\in\mathrm{End}(V_\lambda)$ (the Wolfram routine \texttt{Ee[$\lambda$][i,j]},
defined in Cell 4), simply calls \texttt{JJ[i,j,\{$\lambda$,0\}]} to give the spin-$\lambda/2$
representation matrix of the generator $E_{ij}$, matching \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2 immediately
after the Lax-matrix formula. The single-site Lax matrix (Cell 5, Wolfram routine
\texttt{L[$\lambda$][i,j][u]}) is then
\begin{equation}
  L^{[\lambda]}(u) \;=\; u\,\mathbbm{1} \;-\; h \sum_{i,j} E_{ji}\otimes e_{ij},
  \label{eq:Lax}
\end{equation}
matching \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2; the $(i,j)$ auxiliary-space component implemented by the
routine is precisely the $e_{ij}$-coefficient of \eqref{eq:Lax},
$L^{[\lambda]}_{ij}(u) = u\,\delta_{ij}\,\mathbbm{1} - h\,E_{ji}$.
Here $u$ is left unshifted at this stage; the inhomogeneity shift $u\mapsto u-\theta$ is applied by
the caller when a site is actually instantiated in the two-site chain (\S\ref{sec:twosite} below).
Section E (\S\ref{sec:factorization}) reuses this same single-site $L$, unshifted, to build a
genuinely standalone length-1 chain.

\section{The companion twist and the two-site transfer matrix}
\label{sec:twosite}

The companion-form twist matrix $G$ (Cell 6, with numeric eigenvalues $\kappa_1,\kappa_2$ fixed in
Cell 1) is
\begin{equation}
  G = \begin{pmatrix} \chi_1 & -\chi_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad
  \chi_1=\kappa_1+\kappa_2, \qquad \chi_2=\kappa_1\kappa_2,
  \label{eq:G}
\end{equation}
exactly as in \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.2. The notebook fixes $\theta_1=1/3$, $\theta_2=1/7$,
$h=1$, and a 30-digit numeric twist $\kappa_1=e^{i\zeta(3)}$ with $\kappa_2=1/\kappa_1=\overline{\kappa_1}$
(so $\chi_2=1$). The two-site Lax operators (Cell 8, routines \texttt{L1}, \texttt{L2}) act on
$V_{\lambda_1}\otimes V_{\lambda_2}$ by shifting each site's single-site Lax matrix by its own
inhomogeneity and tensoring with the identity on the other factor,
\[
  L_1(u) = L^{[\lambda_1]}(u-\theta_1)\otimes \mathbbm{1}_{\lambda_2}, \qquad
  L_2(u) = \mathbbm{1}_{\lambda_1}\otimes L^{[\lambda_2]}(u-\theta_2).
\]
The two-site monodromy matrix (Cell 10, memoized routine \texttt{T}) is the ordered product in
auxiliary space,
\begin{equation}
  T(u) = L_1(u)\, L_2(u),
  \label{eq:monodromy}
\end{equation}
with both factors carrying the \emph{same} spectral parameter $u$ (each site's inhomogeneity shift
is already baked into $L_1$, $L_2$); this differs from the single-site quantum-determinant
construction, which shifts the second Lax evaluation by $h$ between two evaluations of the
\emph{same} site's Lax matrix -- an operation that simply does not arise for two distinct physical
sites.

The fundamental, companion-twisted transfer matrix (Cell 11) is
\begin{equation}
  t_{1,1}(u) = \operatorname{tr}_a\!\bigl[G\,T(u)\bigr],
  \label{eq:t11}
\end{equation}
generalizing the single-site formula $t_{1,1}(u)=\operatorname{tr}_a[G\,L^{[\lambda]}(u-\theta)]$ of
\texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2 to the two-site monodromy \eqref{eq:monodromy}; as a matrix on
$V_{\lambda_1}\otimes V_{\lambda_2}$ it is a degree-2 polynomial in $u$. Commutativity of this
family at different spectral parameters is checked in Cell 12 for $\lambda_1,\lambda_2\in\{1,\dots,4\}$,
collapsing $t_{1,1}(u)t_{1,1}(v)-t_{1,1}(v)t_{1,1}(u)$ to zero after simplification. A purely
symbolic variant with a formally \emph{diagonal} twist (Cell 13, routine \texttt{t2diag}) replaces
$G$ by two independent symbolic eigenvalues, written here as $\kappa(1),\kappa(2)$ to emphasize that
they are \textbf{distinct symbols} from the numeric plain twist eigenvalues $\kappa_1,\kappa_2$ --
this variant is used only for a symbolic large-$u$ asymptotics cross-check (Cells 14--17) and plays
no further role. The corresponding total (co-product) generator, $E_{ij}^{\mathrm{tot}} =
E_{ij}\otimes\mathbbm{1} + \mathbbm{1}\otimes E_{ij}$, satisfies the central-charge identity
$E_{11}^{\mathrm{tot}}+E_{22}^{\mathrm{tot}}=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)\,\mathbbm{1}$, checked in Cell 15.

\section{Quantum determinant and the fusion hierarchy}
\label{sec:fusion}

The (untwisted) quantum determinant of the two-site monodromy matrix (Cell 18, routine
\texttt{qdetT}) is
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{qdet}\,T(u) = T_{11}(u)\,T_{22}(u-h) - T_{21}(u)\,T_{12}(u-h),
  \label{eq:qdet}
\end{equation}
generalizing the single-site formula of \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.2. Cell 20 then builds the
Hirota (T-system) fusion hierarchy extending $t_{1,1}$ to general rectangular auxiliary
representations $t_{a,s}(u)$: the boundary conditions $t_{0,s}\equiv t_{a,0}\equiv\mathbbm{1}$; the
antisymmetric fusion
\begin{equation}
  t_{2,1}(u) = \chi_2\,\mathrm{qdet}\,T(u);
  \label{eq:t21}
\end{equation}
and the symmetric fusions via the Hirota recursion
\begin{equation}
  t_{1,s}(u) = t_{1,s-1}(u)\,t_{1,1}\bigl(u+(s-1)h\bigr) - t_{2,1}\bigl(u+(s-1)h\bigr)\,t_{1,s-2}(u),
  \qquad s>1.
  \label{eq:hirota}
\end{equation}
This is an exact generalization of \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.2, which states
\eqref{eq:t21}--\eqref{eq:hirota} for the single-site quantum determinant, to the two-site case.
Section E (\S\ref{sec:factorization}) below rebuilds this exact same hierarchy, transcribed
literally rather than generalized, at $L=1$.

\section{Separation of variables: the \texorpdfstring{$B$}{B}-operator and covector basis}
\label{sec:sov}

Two $\qth$-type normalization factors are defined in Cell 21: the single-site factor
$\qthone(u)=u-\theta_1$ and the two-site product $\qth(u)=(u-\theta_1)(u-\theta_2)$, generalizing
the single-site definition $\qth(u)=u-\theta$ of \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.3. $\qthone$, unused
within Sections A/B themselves, is used directly by Section E's length-1 covector below. Note also
that $\qth$ is \textbf{redefined identically} in Section B (Cell 28, after its own \texttt{ClearAll})
-- harmless while the two definitions agree, but an edit to one without the other would silently
diverge.

The SoV $B$-operator for $L=2$ (Cell 23, routine \texttt{B2}) is simply the bare $(1,1)$ monodromy
element, with no extra twist or trace,
\[
  B_2(u) = T_{11}(u),
\]
matching the single-site convention $B^{[\lambda]}(u)=L^{[\lambda]}_{11}(u-\theta)$ of
\texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.3. The SoV pseudovacuum and covector basis (Cell 25) is built in two
steps. The single-site lowest-weight covector of $V_\lambda^*$'s GT basis (routine \texttt{xSingle})
tensors to give the two-site pseudovacuum,
\[
  \langle x_2(0,0)| = \langle x^{[\lambda_1]}_0|\otimes\langle x^{[\lambda_2]}_0|,
\]
and the full covector basis is obtained (memoized routine \texttt{x2}) by acting with the fused
transfer matrices and normalizing,
\begin{equation}
  \langle x_2(s_1,s_2)| =
  \frac{\langle x_2(0,0)|\;t_{1,s_1}(\theta_1)\;t_{1,s_2}(\theta_2)}
       {\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{s_1}\qth\bigl(\theta_1+(k-1-\lambda_1)h\bigr)\;
        \prod_{k=1}^{s_2}\qth\bigl(\theta_2+(k-1-\lambda_2)h\bigr)}.
  \label{eq:x2}
\end{equation}
Cell 26 verifies that \eqref{eq:x2} diagonalizes $B_2$ with the expected eigenvalue,
\[
  \langle x_2(s_1,s_2)|\, B_2(u) = (u-\theta_1-hs_1)(u-\theta_2-hs_2)\,\langle x_2(s_1,s_2)|,
\]
the product of the two per-site eigenvalues $(u-\theta-sh)$ from \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex}, reflecting
the tensor-product SoV structure. Section E (\S\ref{sec:factorization}) proves that, at $s_2=0$,
\eqref{eq:x2} itself factorizes operatorially through a genuine standalone $L=1$ construction.

\section{The Baxter TQ equation (notebook Section B)}
\label{sec:baxter}

The routines in this part of the notebook jointly solve the transfer-matrix eigenproblem
$t_{1,1}(u)\Psi=\tau(u)\Psi$ together with the Baxter TQ equation
\begin{equation}
  \kappa_1\,a(u)\,q(u+h) + \kappa_2\,\qth(u)\,q(u-h) = \tau(u)\,q(u),
  \label{eq:TQ}
\end{equation}
for the two-site chain, in two stages. First, \texttt{TauEigensystem} diagonalizes the
\emph{genuine} transfer matrix directly -- with no oversized Baxter-matrix proxy and no post-hoc
spurious-root filtering -- to obtain every $(M,n)$-indexed eigenvalue $\tau=\tau_0+J_n$. Then
\texttt{QSolve} solves for the monic degree-$M$ Q-function $q_n(u)$ as a linear nullspace problem at
the now-known $J_n$. \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} does not yet state a Baxter/TQ equation for either
$L=1$ or $L=2$, so none of this section has a paper cross-reference; the full derivation writeup
and validation/regression suite live in \texttt{Experiments/Baxter\_Solver\_L2.wb}. Section C below
builds a full numeric verification of orthogonality of the resulting Q-functions, and Section F
builds the closed functional/operatorial dictionary from them.

\subsection*{Baxter-equation ingredients}

Cell 28 defines, locally so that Section B is self-contained: the Bethe/Baxter coefficient
$a(u) = (u-\theta_1-h\lambda_1)(u-\theta_2-h\lambda_2)$; the $\qth$ factor
$\qth(u)=(u-\theta_1)(u-\theta_2)$, an identical redefinition of the one from \S\ref{sec:sov}; the
$J$-independent part of the transfer-matrix eigenvalue,
\[
  \tau_0(u) = (\kappa_1+\kappa_2)u^2 - (\theta_1+\theta_2)(\kappa_1+\kappa_2)u
    - u\,h\bigl(\kappa_1(\lambda_1+\lambda_2-M)+\kappa_2 M\bigr),
\]
which by construction has zero constant term, so the full eigenvalue is $\tau=\tau_0+J$ with $J$ the
constant term; and a helper \texttt{qpoly[clist,uu]} that evaluates a polynomial given ascending
coefficients $\{c_0,\dots,c_M\}$ at a point, avoiding an indeterminate $0^0$.

\subsection*{The Baxter matrix and transfer-matrix coefficients}

\texttt{BaxterMatrix} at fixed $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,M)$ (Cell 29) builds the fixed linear operator,
in the monic-degree-$M$-polynomial coefficient basis, whose nullspace at a known $J$ gives the
Q-function's coefficients. For $k=0,\dots,M$ its $k$-th column is obtained by expanding
\begin{equation}
  O[u^k] = \kappa_1\,a(u)(u+h)^k + \kappa_2\,\qth(u)(u-h)^k - \tau_0(u)\,u^k
  \label{eq:baxtercol}
\end{equation}
as a polynomial in $u$; the routine hard-asserts that the degree-$(M{+}1)$ and degree-$(M{+}2)$
coefficients of \eqref{eq:baxtercol} vanish identically for every column, and the surviving
degree-$0,\dots,M$ coefficients become the columns of the resulting $(M{+}1)\times(M{+}1)$ matrix.
Separately, \texttt{t2Coeffs[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$]} (Cell 30, memoized, reused directly by
Section D's left-eigenvector construction) extracts the coefficient matrices $C_0,C_1,C_2$ of
$t_{1,1}(u) = C_0 + C_1 u + C_2 u^2$ entrywise, confirming $C_2=(\kappa_1+\kappa_2)\mathbbm{1}$. The
predicted dimension of the weight-$M$ subspace of $V_{\lambda_1}\otimes V_{\lambda_2}$ (Cell 31,
routine \texttt{dPred}) is
\[
  d_{\mathrm{pred}}(M,\lambda_1,\lambda_2) = \min(M,\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\lambda_1+\lambda_2-M) + 1,
\]
the exact multiplicity that both \texttt{TauEigensystem} and Section D's \texttt{TauEigensystemLeft}
must reproduce per $M$-sector.

\subsection*{Diagonalizing the genuine transfer matrix}

\texttt{TauEigensystem[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$]} (Cell 31, memoized) diagonalizes the genuine
transfer matrix $t_{1,1}(u)$ directly. Since $C_0,C_1,C_2$ pairwise commute, it diagonalizes a
generic linear combination $C_0+rC_1$, trying several candidate values of $r$ until the eigenvectors
have full rank $d=(\lambda_1+1)(\lambda_2+1)$ with $d$ distinct eigenvalues. For each eigenvector
$v$ it extracts $t_0 = v\cdot C_0 v/v\cdot v$, $t_1 = v\cdot C_1 v/v\cdot v$, hard-aborting if either
residual $\|C_0v-t_0v\|,\|C_1v-t_1v\|$ exceeds $10^{-15}$ -- this residual check is itself the
genuineness test. The weight label $M$ is read off algebraically from $t_1$ by inverting $\tau_0$'s
$u^1$ coefficient,
\begin{equation}
  M = \frac{-(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\kappa_1+\kappa_2) - h\kappa_1(\lambda_1+\lambda_2) - t_1}
           {h(\kappa_2-\kappa_1)},
  \label{eq:Mextract}
\end{equation}
hard-asserted within $10^{-10}$ of an integer, and $J=t_0$ directly. States within each $M$-sector
are sorted ascending by $(\operatorname{Re}J,\operatorname{Im}J)$ to assign the index $n$, with
each sector's count hard-asserted to equal $d_{\mathrm{pred}}(M,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$ exactly. The
result is an association keyed by $(M,n)$ giving $(J_n,\Psi_{M,n})$, with $\Psi_{M,n}$ normalized to
last component $1$. As a representative evaluation, the notebook's own kernel state gives
\begin{verbatim}
KeySort[TauEigensystem[1, 1]][[All, "J"]] // N
(* <|{0,0} -> 0.5664055180884988+1.3769645669711723 I,
     {1,0} -> -0.778123966451158+0. I,
     {1,1} -> 1.1900552523337025+0. I,
     {2,0} -> 0.5664055180884988-1.3769645669711723 I|> *)
\end{verbatim}

Bounds-checked accessors (Cell 32) then give the eigenvector $\Psi_{M,n}=\texttt{Psi}(M,n)$ and the
full eigenvalue polynomial $\tau_{M,n}(u)=\tau_0(u)+J_n$; if $n$ is within the gross bound
$0\le n\le M$ but exceeds the actual multiplicity $d_{\mathrm{pred}}(M,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)-1$, a
spurious-state message fires and a missing value is returned.

\subsection*{Solving for the Q-function}

\texttt{QSolve[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$][M,n]} (Cell 33, memoized) solves \eqref{eq:TQ} as a
\emph{linear} problem, since $J_n$ (hence $\tau$) is already known: it finds the monic degree-$M$
polynomial $q_n$ satisfying $(\mathrm{BaxterMatrix}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,M) - J_n\mathbbm{1})\, q_n = 0$,
a matrix that is singular by construction. The would-be-zero entries of this matrix come out as
\emph{precision-tracked} zeros, which a bare nullspace computation treats as unknown rather than
confirmed-zero -- specifically causing a failure in the $M=0$ sector under either the default
tolerance or an explicit tolerance of $10^{-10}$ alone. The fix used inside \texttt{QSolve} is to
chop the matrix at threshold $10^{-12}$ \emph{before} computing the nullspace, keeping an explicit
tolerance of $10^{-10}$ as additional headroom. As a representative, regression-critical example:
\begin{verbatim}
QSolve[2, 1][2, 0]
(* {1.03464899078135229594035121+0.381869568380946283899575941 I,
    -2.37022278949001677248172619-0.20048970475590097847093779 I,
    1.00000000000000000000000000+0.\times10^-27 I} *)
\end{verbatim}
Finally, \texttt{Q1[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$][M,n][u]} (Cell 34) assembles the actual Baxter
Q-function value,
\[
  Q_{M,n}(u) = \kappa_1^{u/h}\, q_n(u),
\]
consistent with $\tau_{M,n}$ by construction. This is the object that Section C tests for
orthogonality and that Section F builds the functional CG-overlap determinant from.

\section{The FSoV scalar product and the Gram matrix (Section C)}
\label{sec:fsov}

Section C ports the Functional Separation of Variables (FSoV) machinery of
\texttt{Experiments/FSoV\_L2.wb} into this notebook and uses it to verify orthogonality of the
numeric Baxter Q-functions \texttt{Q1} built in \S\ref{sec:baxter}, over a full representation at
once (all $M$-sectors, including cross-$M$ pairs). See
\url{docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-16-fsov-scalar-product-design.md}. Since
\texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} does not yet state an FSoV scalar product, none of this section has a
direct paper cross-reference.

For each site $i=1,2$, Cell 36's routine \texttt{mu[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$][i][u]} is a rational
measure with simple poles on that site's inhomogeneity tower $\theta_i+hk$,
$k=0,\dots,\lambda_i$, carrying an extra cross-site oscillatory factor,
\[
  \mu_1(u) = \frac{N_1\,\bigl(1-e^{2\pi i(u-\theta_2)/h}\bigr)}
                  {\prod_{k=0}^{\lambda_1}(u-\theta_1-hk)\prod_{k=0}^{\lambda_2}(u-\theta_2-hk)},
\]
symmetric for site 2. The normalization $N_1$ (Cell 37, routines \texttt{Nn}/\texttt{nn}) is fixed
so that the resulting bracket pairing (Cell 38, routine \texttt{br}) --- the residue sum of
$f\cdot\mu_i$ over site $i$'s whole pole tower,
\[
  \langle f\rangle_i = 2\pi i \sum_{\text{poles } p \text{ of } \mu_i}\operatorname{Res}_{u=p}\bigl[f(u)\,\mu_i(u)\bigr],
\]
--- gives the right scale for the determinant below. Cell 39's routine
\texttt{det[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$][f]} assembles the $2\times2$, Wronskian-type determinant
\begin{equation}
  \det[\lambda_1,\lambda_2][f] = \frac{1}{f(\theta_1)\,f(\theta_2)\,(\theta_2-\theta_1)}
  \det\begin{pmatrix} \langle f\rangle_1 & \langle uf\rangle_1 \\ \langle f\rangle_2 & \langle uf\rangle_2 \end{pmatrix},
  \label{eq:fsovdet}
\end{equation}
which \emph{is} the FSoV scalar product: for $f=Q_A(u)Q_B(u)$, \eqref{eq:fsovdet} must vanish for
$A\neq B$ and be nonzero for $A=B$. Cell 40 is a port-fidelity check: a verbatim transcription of
\texttt{FSoV\_L2.wb}'s own cached closed form for $\det[1,1][f(u)z^{u/h}]$ with $\theta_1,\theta_2,h$
kept symbolic is compared against a fresh evaluation of \eqref{eq:fsovdet} with the same
substitution; live kernel state confirms the difference is exactly $0$.

Cell 41's \texttt{statesOf[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$]} reads the sorted $\{M,n\}$ keys off
\texttt{TauEigensystem}'s memoized cache (e.g.\ \texttt{statesOf[2,1]} $=\{(0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(2,0),
(2,1),(3,0)\}$), and \texttt{GramMatrix} assembles the full Gram matrix over a representation,
\begin{equation}
  G_{(M,n),(M',n')} = \det[\lambda_1,\lambda_2]\bigl[Q_1(M,n)(u)\,Q_1(M',n')(u)\bigr],
  \label{eq:gram}
\end{equation}
covering same-$M$ degeneracy and cross-$M$ pairs simultaneously. The concrete instance
\texttt{G21}$=\mathrm{GramMatrix}(2,1)$ is a $6\times6$ matrix over weight sectors
$M=0,1,2,3$ with $d_{\mathrm{pred}}(M,2,1)=1,2,2,1$ -- two genuinely 2-dimensional degenerate
sectors, a nontrivial test of the scalar product's ability to resolve degenerate states. Cell 45
hard-asserts that the maximum off-diagonal magnitude is below $10^{-15}$ and the minimum diagonal
magnitude is above it; live kernel state gives
\[
  \max|G_{\text{off-diag}}| = 5.065342385207116\times10^{-34}, \qquad
  \min|G_{\text{diag}}| = 3.0051232400647923,
\]
i.e.\ orthogonality vanishing to $\sim33$ digits (well within the notebook's $\sim30$-digit working
precision) and genuinely nonzero norms on all 6 states, including both degenerate sectors.

\section{Left eigenvectors and biorthogonality (Section D)}
\label{sec:lefteig}

Section D builds an \emph{independent} left-eigenvector construction (it never edits
\texttt{TauEigensystem}) and uses it to form a bilinear pairing $\langle\Psi|\Psi\rangle$, comparing
it against Section C's scalar product. See
\url{docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-16-fsov-left-eigenvector-ratio-design.md}; no predicted
formula for the resulting ratio exists yet in \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} -- this section reports
values rather than asserting a predicted one, though the observed ratio is consistently $1$.

\texttt{TauEigensystemLeft[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$]} (Cell 47) diagonalizes
$\operatorname{Transpose}(C_0+rC_1)$ for its own generic $r$ (chosen, as in
\texttt{TauEigensystem}, to give full rank and distinct eigenvalues), then matches each left
eigenvector to an existing $\{M,n\}$ state by comparing eigenvalues: since $C_2=(\kappa_1+\kappa_2)
\mathbbm{1}$ exactly and $t_{1,1}(r)\Psi_{M,n}=\tau_{M,n}(r)\Psi_{M,n}$ for the right eigenvector,
the target combo-eigenvalue for state $\{M,n\}$ is $\tau_{M,n}(r)-(\kappa_1+\kappa_2)r^2$. The
match is hard-asserted to lie within $10^{-10}$, to never re-use a left-eigenvector index, and to
have a non-vanishing last component before normalizing to it; the total count is hard-asserted to
equal $d=(\lambda_1+1)(\lambda_2+1)$. A smoke test (Cell 48) confirms
\texttt{TauEigensystemLeft[2,1]} reproduces the same 6 keys as \texttt{statesOf[2,1]}
(\S\ref{sec:fsov}) via this fully independent construction. \texttt{PsiLeft[$\lambda_1$,$\lambda_2$][M,n]}
(Cell 49) is the bounds-checked accessor over this cache, the left-eigenvector counterpart of
\texttt{Psi}.

The correctness check on this whole construction is biorthogonality (Cell 50): a genuine left/right
eigenvector pair of a diagonalizable operator with simple spectrum must satisfy
$\mathrm{PsiLeft}(M,n)\cdot\Psi(M',n')=0$ for $(M,n)\neq(M',n')$. Live kernel state confirms the
maximum off-diagonal pairing is exactly $0$ over all 30 off-diagonal pairs of $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)=(2,1)$.
Given biorthogonality, Cell 51 defines the plain bilinear (non-conjugated) diagonal pairing
\[
  \mathrm{braket}(M,n) = \langle\Psi_{M,n}|\Psi_{M,n}\rangle := \mathrm{PsiLeft}(M,n)\cdot\Psi(M,n),
\]
and, dividing by the corresponding diagonal entry of Section C's Gram matrix
$G_{(M,n),(M,n)}$ (only meaningful for the fixed representation $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)=(2,1)$, since
it hard-codes the globals \texttt{states21}/\texttt{G21}), the ratio
$\mathrm{normRatio}(M,n) = \mathrm{braket}(M,n)/G_{(M,n),(M,n)}$. Cell 52's final report tabulates
this for all 6 states of $(2,1)$; live kernel state shows, e.g.\ for $(M,n)=(0,0)$,
$\mathrm{braket} = 2.9090642893150832631 - 5.804620063565933843\,i$, numerically identical to the
corresponding Gram diagonal entry -- i.e.\ $\mathrm{normRatio}=1$ to the tracked precision, for
every one of the 6 states. This equality of the two independently-constructed scalar products,
$\langle\Psi|\Psi\rangle = \det[Q,Q]$ on the diagonal, is the headline finding of Section D.

\section{Operatorial factorization of the SoV basis (Section E)}
\label{sec:factorization}

Section E builds a genuinely \emph{standalone} length-1 fused transfer-matrix hierarchy and SoV
covector -- the paper's actual $L=1$ construction, built here entirely independently of Section A's
two-site $t_{1,1}$/$x_2$ (reusing only the single-site $L^{[\lambda]}$, $G$, $\qthone$,
$\langle x_0|$, and $\dim V_\lambda$) -- and uses it to prove that the $s_2=0$ slice of the two-site
SoV basis \eqref{eq:x2} factorizes through a rank-1 site-2 ``passthrough'' eigenvalue. See
\url{docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-16-xxx-sov-factorization-functional-overlap-design.md};
Sections A--D are read-only from this point on.

Cell 54 rebuilds the entire length-1 fusion hierarchy $t_{1,1}^{[1]}(u)=\operatorname{tr}_a[G\,
L^{[\lambda]}(u-\theta_1)]$, quantum determinant $\mathrm{qdet}\,L^{[\lambda]}(u) =
L^{[\lambda]}_{11}(u-\theta_1)L^{[\lambda]}_{22}(u-\theta_1-h) -
L^{[\lambda]}_{21}(u-\theta_1)L^{[\lambda]}_{12}(u-\theta_1-h)$, and Hirota recursion for
$t_{1,s}^{[1]}$, in exactly the form \eqref{eq:t21}--\eqref{eq:hirota} take, but applied to a single
site rather than the two-site monodromy $T$ -- a literal transcription of
\texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.2, not a generalization (Cell 55 is a shape smoke test). Cell 56 then
defines the length-1 SoV $B$-operator $B_1(u)=L^{[\lambda]}_{11}(u-\theta_1)$ and covector
\begin{equation}
  \langle x^{[1]}_s| = \frac{\langle x_0|\,t_{1,s}^{[1]}(\theta_1)}{\prod_{p=1}^{s}\qthone(\theta_1-\lambda h+(p-1)h)},
  \label{eq:xL1}
\end{equation}
a literal transcription of \texttt{paper/xxxCG.tex} \S2.3's single-site SoV covector formula. Cell
57 verifies \eqref{eq:xL1} is a genuine left eigencovector of $B_1$ with eigenvalue $u-\theta_1-sh$,
symbolically in $u$ for $\lambda=1,2,3$ (live kernel state: the check collapses to the literal list
$\{0\}$).

The factorization statement itself (Cell 58) is that
\begin{equation}
  \langle x_2(s_1,0)| = c\,\Bigl(\langle x^{[1]}_{s_1}|_{\lambda_1}\otimes \langle x_0|_{\lambda_2}\Bigr),
  \label{eq:factorization}
\end{equation}
for a scalar $c$ that is checked, over the full sweep $\lambda_1,\lambda_2\in\{1,2,3\}$,
$s_1\in\{0,\dots,\lambda_1\}$, to be (i) genuinely constant across the tensor product's nonzero
components (spread below $10^{-15}$), and (ii) equal to the closed form
\begin{equation}
  c = \mathrm{cPredCorr}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,s_1) = \prod_{k=1}^{s_1}
    \frac{\theta_1-\theta_2+(k-1)h-\lambda_2 h}{\theta_1-\theta_2+(k-1)h-\lambda_1 h}.
  \label{eq:cpredcorr}
\end{equation}
This corrected scalar \eqref{eq:cpredcorr} supersedes an earlier naive guess (a trivial identity
site-2 passthrough) that failed; it reduces to $c=1$ at $\lambda_1=\lambda_2$. Live kernel state
confirms, e.g., $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,s_1)=(1,1,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$ give $c=1$ exactly, while
$(1,2,1)$ gives $c=38/17$ exactly matching \eqref{eq:cpredcorr} -- with the ratio $\mu_{\mathrm{eff}}
:= c/\mathrm{cPredCorr}$ hard-asserted (not merely reported) to equal $1$ to the noise floor, over
every case in the sweep.

The physics behind \eqref{eq:cpredcorr} is made explicit in Cell 60: the site-2 pseudovacuum
$\langle x_0|_{\lambda_2}$ is itself a left eigencovector of the site-2 Lax factor appearing in
$t_{1,s_1}(\theta_1)$ at each of the $s_1$ fused steps, with eigenvalue
\begin{equation}
  \mu_{\mathrm{Site2}}(\lambda_2,s_1) = \prod_{k=1}^{s_1}\bigl(\theta_1-\theta_2+(k-1)h-\lambda_2 h\bigr),
  \label{eq:musite2}
\end{equation}
which is why a naive ``trivial passthrough'' guess failed: the site-2 GT lowest-weight eigenvalue of
$L^{[\lambda_2]}_{11}(\theta_1-\theta_2)$ is nontrivial at each fused step (shifted by $(k-1)h$ from
the internal Hirota recursion), not $1$. This is verified directly at the numerator level (before
any $\langle x^{[1]}_{s_1}|$ normalization), hard-asserting both constancy and agreement with
\eqref{eq:musite2}; live kernel state confirms, e.g., $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,s_1)=(1,2,1)$ gives
$\mu_{\mathrm{val}}=\mu_{\mathrm{pred}}=-1.8095238095238095$ exactly, over the whole sweep.

\section{The functional Clebsch--Gordan overlap dictionary (Section F)}
\label{sec:dictionary}

Section F is the notebook's headline result. It sets up a length-1 reduction of Section C's FSoV
measure/bracket (renormalized rather than merely restricted), builds a purely \emph{functional}
object from the numeric Baxter Q-functions of \S\ref{sec:baxter}, builds a purely
\emph{operatorial} object from rotating the SoV pseudovacuum by a one-parameter unipotent group
element, and exhibits a closed dictionary equating the two under a single linear rescaling of the
parameter. See
\url{docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-16-xxx-sov-factorization-functional-overlap-design.md}; none
of this section has a paper cross-reference, since it is the notebook's own novel result.

\subsection{The 1-site FSoV determinant}

Cell 62 defines the length-1 measure $\mu_1(u) = N_1/\prod_{k=0}^{\lambda}(u-\theta_1-hk)$, with
$N_1$ \emph{derived} (not guessed) by forcing the leading ($\theta_1$-node) weight
$w_0 := 2\pi i\,\operatorname{Res}_{u=\theta_1}\mu_1(u)$ to equal $1$, so that the bracket
$\mathrm{br}_1[f] = 2\pi i\sum_{k=0}^{\lambda}\operatorname{Res}_{u=\theta_1+hk}\bigl[f(u)\mu_1(u)\bigr]$
satisfies $\mathrm{br}_1[f] = f(\theta_1) + (\text{higher-tower terms})$. For $\lambda\ge1$ the
residues of $\mu_1$ alone sum to $0$ (so $\mathrm{br}_1[1]=0$ identically), which is why the correct
normalization statement to check is $w_0=1$ rather than $\mathrm{br}_1[1]=1$; Cell 63 hard-asserts
both $w_0=1$ and $\mathrm{br}_1[1]=0$ for $\lambda=1,2,3$ (live kernel state:
$N_1(1)=i/(2\pi)$, $N_1(2)=-i/\pi$, $N_1(3)=3i/\pi$), and Cell 64 confirms $\mathrm{br}_1$ acting on
a generic symbolic $g(u)$ reproduces the full weighted tower sum $\sum_j w_j\,g(\theta_1+hj)$
exactly, not merely on its leading term.

The 1-site FSoV determinant (Cell 68) is the 1-site reduction of Section C's $\det[\lambda_1,
\lambda_2][f]$, which divides by $f$ at \emph{both} nodes $\theta_1,\theta_2$; only the
$\theta_1$-node survives at 1 site,
\begin{equation}
  \det{}_1[\lambda_1][f] = \frac{\mathrm{br}_1[\lambda_1][f]}{f(\theta_1)}.
  \label{eq:det1}
\end{equation}
Applied to $k^{u/h}Q_1(M,n)(u)$ (recall $Q_1$ already carries $\kappa_1^{u/h}$, so
$k^{u/h}Q_1(u) = (k\kappa_1)^{u/h}q_n(u)$, whose value at $u=\theta_1$ is a fractional power of
$k\kappa_1$), the $1/f(\theta_1)$ normalization in \eqref{eq:det1} exactly cancels that fractional
prefactor, leaving
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{FLdet}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[M,n][k] := \det{}_1[\lambda_1]\bigl[k^{u/h}Q_1(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[M,n](u)\bigr]
  \label{eq:fldet}
\end{equation}
a clean degree-$\lambda_1$ polynomial in $k$ with leading (constant) term exactly $1$, by
construction of $w_0=1$. This is precisely why Section F is built from $\det_1$ rather than the bare
bracket $\mathrm{br}_1$: the bare bracket would retain the fractional prefactor.

\subsection{The operatorial overlap}

On the operatorial side, Cell 65 defines the finite unipotent rotation of a single-site generator,
built as an \emph{exact truncated series} rather than a formal matrix exponential (which would
return unevaluated here, since $E_{ab}$ is nilpotent of order at most $\lambda+1$ on the
finite-dimensional representation),
\begin{equation}
  g_{a,b}(\varphi) = \mathbbm{1} + \sum_{m=1}^{\lambda}\frac{\varphi^m}{m!}\,E_{ab}^m,
  \label{eq:grot}
\end{equation}
and the operatorial overlap
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{OP}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[a,b][M,n](\varphi) = \Bigl(\langle x_0|_{\lambda_1}\,g_{a,b}(\varphi)\Bigr)\otimes\langle x_0|_{\lambda_2}
  \;\cdot\;\Psi_{M,n}.
  \label{eq:op}
\end{equation}
(In this kernel, \texttt{MatrixPower}$[\cdot,0]$ on a singular matrix does not auto-reduce to the
identity even for the literal integer $0$ -- it stays symbolically unevaluated and raises a
message -- so \eqref{eq:grot}'s $m=0$ term is split off explicitly rather than summed from $m=0$.)
Cell 66 determines which single-site generator, $E_{12}$ or $E_{21}$, actually produces
$\varphi$-dependence in \eqref{eq:op} -- the lowest-weight covector $\langle x_0|$ annihilates the
other one by construction. On the test case $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)=(2,1)$, state $(M,n)=(1,0)$,
live kernel state confirms $\mathrm{OP}$ built from $E_{12}$ is exactly $\varphi$-independent
(constant $=1$) while $\mathrm{OP}$ built from $E_{21}$ genuinely depends on $\varphi$ -- fixing the
nontrivial generator to $\{a,b\}=\{2,1\}$ (routine \texttt{genNontrivial}), and Cell 67 defines
$\mathrm{OPg}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[M,n](\varphi) := \mathrm{OP}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[2,1][M,n](\varphi)$
as the fixed downstream object, a polynomial of degree at most $\lambda_1$ in $\varphi$.

\subsection{The closed dictionary}

The discovery step (Cell 69) tests $\lambda_1=1$ universality on representation $(1,2)$, all 6
states: both $\mathrm{FLdet}(1,2)[M,n](k) = 1 + D_{M,n}\,k$ and
$\mathrm{OPg}(1,2)[M,n](\varphi) = 1 + c_{1,M,n}\,\varphi$ are degree-1 polynomials with constant
term hard-asserted to be exactly $1$ for every state (a structural consequence of the two
normalizations, not a coincidence); the ratio
$\alpha_{M,n} := D_{M,n}/c_{1,M,n}$ is then hard-asserted to be the \emph{same} constant across all
6 states (spread below $10^{-12}$) -- this constancy, not the constant-term-1 fact, is the actual
universality content. Live kernel state gives the universal value exactly,
\begin{equation}
  \alpha = -1.
  \label{eq:alpha}
\end{equation}
Cell 70 then validates, with $\varphi(k) := \alpha k$ using the single fixed $\alpha$ from
\eqref{eq:alpha}, the full identity
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{FLdet}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[M,n](k) = \mathrm{OPg}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)[M,n]\bigl(\alpha k\bigr)
  \label{eq:dictionary}
\end{equation}
across the complete representation sweep $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\in\{(1,1),(2,1),(1,2),(2,2),(3,1),
(1,3)\}$ and every state of each, hard-asserting that
$\mathrm{Chop}[\mathrm{Simplify}[\mathrm{LHS}-\mathrm{RHS}],10^{-15}]$ is the literal value $0$ for
every one of them -- this is the real content, testing that the tower weights $w_m$ (from $\mu_1$)
match $\mathrm{OPg}$'s $\varphi^m$ Taylor coefficients (built from $E_{21}^m$ via $g_{a,b}$) under
the single rescaling $\alpha$, for every $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,M,n)$. Live kernel state confirms all
41 residuals (summing $4+6+6+9+8+8$ states over the 6 representations,
$(\lambda_1+1)(\lambda_2+1)$ each) are the literal symbolic value $0$, not merely numerically small.
Cell 71's final report collects the dictionary: generator $E_{21}$, $\varphi(k)=\alpha k$ with
$\alpha=-1$ exactly, normalization $\det_1$, over the 6-representation, 41-state sweep, with maximum
residual exactly $0$.

\section{Known gotchas and non-obvious conventions}

The cell-output display of this notebook currently renders empty via the notebook-reading tools,
across all six sections -- the same documented tool-capture artifact recorded for the original A+B
version of this notebook, carried over unchanged after the Section C--F merge and confirmed not to
be a correctness problem by direct kernel-state inspection and by the recorded activity log of a
clean fresh-kernel run immediately preceding this documentation pass.

Two symbols are defined more than once with agreeing but independent formulas, a latent source of
future silent divergence: the formal, purely symbolic indexed twist $\kappa(i)$ used only in the
diagonal-twist asymptotics cross-check of \S\ref{sec:twosite} is unrelated to the numeric plain
twist eigenvalues $\kappa_1,\kappa_2$ used everywhere else; and $\qth(u)=(u-\theta_1)(u-\theta_2)$
is defined independently in both \S\ref{sec:sov} and \S\ref{sec:baxter} (the latter after its own
\texttt{ClearAll}). Several routines throughout the notebook are self-memoizing
(\texttt{ps}, \texttt{dim}, the generator routines, \texttt{T}, \texttt{x2}, \texttt{t2Coeffs},
\texttt{TauEigensystem}, \texttt{TauEigensystemLeft}, \texttt{QSolve}, $\langle x^{[1]}_s|$,
$N_1$) -- after fixing any upstream definition, the defining cell and everything downstream that
used the stale cache must be re-run.

Section D's $\mathrm{normRatio}$ is scoped to the single fixed representation
$(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)=(2,1)$: it looks up its state's index in, and divides by, the global Gram
matrix \texttt{G21}/\texttt{states21} rather than recomputing them, so it is not a general-purpose
$(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$-parametrized routine despite its argument list. All bilinear pairings in
this notebook -- $\det[\lambda_1,\lambda_2][f]$, $\langle f\rangle_i$, the Gram matrix, and
$\langle\Psi_{M,n}|\Psi_{M,n}\rangle$ -- use \emph{no complex conjugation}, a deliberate convention
consistent throughout (the companion twist $G$ and the Q-functions are genuinely non-Hermitian
objects here), not an oversight.

Section F's clean-polynomial property for $\mathrm{FLdet}$ relies on the prefactor cancellation
described around \eqref{eq:fldet} above -- using $\det_1$ rather than the bare bracket
$\mathrm{br}_1$ is essential, not a stylistic choice. Section F's $g_{a,b}(\varphi)$ construction
also works around a kernel quirk: \texttt{MatrixPower}$[\cdot,0]$ on a singular matrix does not
auto-simplify to the identity even for the literal integer exponent $0$, so the identity term of
\eqref{eq:grot} is added explicitly rather than included in the sum.

Finally, the notebook's numeric precision budget is set once, in Cell 1: $h=1$, and a 30-digit
numeric twist $\kappa_1=e^{i\zeta(3)}$ (with $\kappa_2=1/\kappa_1=\overline{\kappa_1}$). All hard
tolerance thresholds scattered through Sections B--F ($10^{-10}$, $10^{-12}$, $10^{-15}$) and all
reported residuals ($10^{-20}$ to $10^{-34}$ across the various sections) are calibrated against
this $\sim30$-digit working precision, not machine precision -- substituting a lower-precision
numeric twist would require re-checking every one of these thresholds.

\end{document}
